checks.a_analysis: The output content was compared meticulously against the source PDF. All headers, list items, and table cell contents in the JSON output have a direct basis in the source document. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were identified. The table captions and summaries are acceptable AI-generated additions for accessibility.

The source document does not contain phone numbers, email addresses, or physical addresses. It mentions 'NC OneMap' which is correctly reflected. No contact information has been fabricated in the output.

All three pages of the source document are represented in the output. Goal 1 (sub-items 1.1, 1.2, 1.3 on page 1 and 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 1.8 on page 2) and Goal 2 (sub-items 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.4 on page 3) are fully present. Item 1.4 is missing from the source document itself, and its absence in the output is therefore not a defect.
The output contains three table elements corresponding to the tabular structures on each of the three pages. Row counts for each objective (1.1 through 2.4) match the source document exactly.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section and table cell was checked for completeness. No significant text content has been omitted. The output successfully captures the detailed descriptions and committee actions associated with each objective.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: Minor variations in punctuation (e.g., use of hyphens vs. en-dashes in acronyms) and OCR-related spacing are present but do not alter the meaning of the document. The wording across all goals and objectives is faithful to the source.

checks.a_analysis: The text 'Goal 1. Improve, expand, and support statewide geospatial data and applications.' appears twice in the JSON output (heading-3 and heading-4). However, this text is intentionally repeated in the source document at the top of Page 1 and the top of Page 2 to provide context for the table that continues across pages. As this reflects the source document's design, it is not a defect.

The document title and main section headings are all assigned as level 3 headings (H3). While this creates a flat hierarchy, it does not violate the structural integrity by skipping levels (e.g., jumping from H1 to H3). The levels are consistent throughout the document, which is acceptable as a normalization for accessibility.

The source document organizes objectives (1.1, 1.2, etc.) as colored bars spanning a two-column grid. The JSON conversion faithfully represents this by identifying these bars as header rows with a 'colspan' of 2. The data rows underneath are correctly split into two columns, matching the source layout. The separation of tables across the three document pages is also correctly preserved.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom flow of the source PDF, progressing from the title and acronyms through the tables for Goal 1 and Goal 2 in the sequence they appear across the three pages.
The 'Committee Acronyms' section is correctly converted from a vertical list in the source into an unordered list structure in the JSON, maintaining the pairing of acronyms and full titles.
The source document contains no hyperlinks, and the output correctly reflects this without injecting any fabricated or broken links.

checks.a_analysis: The source document presents each objective (e.g., 1.1, 1.2, 1.3) as a distinct heading bar immediately followed by its associated data row(s). This creates a clear visual and logical flow where each objective is contextually linked to its specific implementation details. The HTML output rearranges this by consolidating multiple objective headings into a single 'thead' section of a large table, followed by all data rows in the 'tbody'. This significantly disrupts the visual organization and flow, making it difficult for a user to determine which implementation row belongs to which objective heading, especially in 'Part 1' where there are three headers but four data rows.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 shows Objective 1.1 as a blue header followed by 2 rows of text, then Objective 1.2 as a blue header followed by 1 row, and Objective 1.3 as a blue header followed by 1 row.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML produces a single table 'Goal 1 Objectives and Actions - Part 1' where the text for objectives 1.1, 1.2, and 1.3 are stacked as three consecutive header rows in the <thead>, followed by all four data rows in the <tbody>.
checks.b_evidence_location: Main content area, Goal 1 tables (Objectives 1.1-1.3, 1.5-1.8, and 2.1-2.4).

checks.a_analysis: The tables in the HTML are technically well-formed with visible borders, distinct headers (using bold text and background shading), and aligned columns. While the logical structure of the content within the tables is flawed due to the layout changes noted in V1, the tables themselves are not visually corrupted (no overlapping text or missing borders). A sighted user can read the data cells.

The source PDF contains no meaningful images, logos, or charts; it consists entirely of text, layout boxes, and colored bars. The HTML faithfully represents the text content without needing image tags.

There is no genuine body content duplication. The repetition of the 'Goal 1' heading in the HTML matches its appearance at the top of both pages 1 and 2 in the source PDF. The appearance of titles in the Table of Contents versus the main body is expected accessible design and not considered a defect.
